_meta.note: Field name is 'verses' throughout (complete arrays, not samples). anchor_vc_id is a member of each VCG's verses array. CC resolves provisional_codes to final group_ids in DB.

vcgs.description: Shame as unjust wound from aggression, circumstance, and loss. Verses where shame operates as an undeserved inner wound — inflicted by the aggression of enemies, the opposition of adversaries, the social pressure of reproach from surrounding peoples, or the circumstantial force of natural disaster and bereavement. Includes the perpetual all-day covering of disgrace, the face that cannot be lifted, the community's shame from surrounding nations, the mother stripped of her children.

Shame from disappointed trust in human powers. Verses where shame arises from misplaced reliance on human allies or earthly resources that prove incapable of delivering — Egypt, Cush, political alliances failing, the trusted object collapsing. The inner wound of having trusted wrongly and been left exposed.

Shame borne for God's sake and by the righteous sufferer. Verses where shame is consciously carried by the innocent or righteous as the cost of faithfulness — the servant enduring humiliation without flinching, the apostles counted worthy to suffer dishonour for Christ, Jesus despising the cross's shame, Paul's freedom from inner shame grounded in divine authority.

Shame promised removal and transformation. Verses where God explicitly promises to remove, forget, or transform the shame covering his people — Israel's youthful shame wholly forgotten, dishonour giving way to double portion and rejoicing, the outcast's shame turned into praise and renown. Future orientation: God's restorative act reversing the unjust wound.

Shame averted by trust, hope, and divine protection. Verses where shame is the present inner threat and trust in God is the present shield — the righteous not put to shame because their trust is well-placed, divine help displacing the inner collapse, Paul's hope directing his will away from shame, suffering for Christ recast as occasion for praise.

Shame invoked on enemies as just reversal of their hostility. Verses where the psalmist or community calls shame upon enemies, adversaries, and persecutors as the fitting reversal of hostile intent — shame clothing the malicious, enemies suddenly overtaken by disgrace, God vindicating the sufferer by putting opponents to shame. The psalmist's praise when enemies are shamed confirms God's help.

Shame from idolatry and false religious trust. Verses where shame falls as the direct consequence of idolatry, false worship, or misplaced religious confidence — idol-makers destined for shame when handiwork proves breathless, the inner bankruptcy of trusting in images exposed when the true God acts, collective confusion and disorientation of those whose identity is built on falsehood.

Shame as natural fruit of personal and relational moral failure. Verses where shame is the direct, natural consequence of identifiable moral failures — pride, folly, negligence, deception, adultery, laziness, parental dishonour, wickedness. The moral order is visible: specific inner failures produce disgrace as their natural social and inner fruit.

Absence of shame: the seared conscience and moral collapse. Verses evidencing the loss of the capacity for shame — those who cannot blush, who feel no shame after abominations, whose inner moral sense is wholly extinguished. The brazen forehead, repeated commission without contrition, hardened dismissal of any possibility of judgment. Judgment follows because the moral corrective has failed.

Corrective shame: blameless conduct, communal discipline, and shame as moral prod. Verses where shame is deployed instrumentally as a corrective — irreproachable conduct causing opponents' shame by exposing their accusations as groundless, communal isolation producing corrective shame within the disobedient, Paul directing shame at a community to awaken conscience, God shaming the wise through the foolish.

Guilt-shame before the holy: the face that cannot be lifted. Verses where shame arises from guilt-consciousness before God's holiness — the inner collapse of dignity in the divine presence, inability to lift the face, blushing that cannot be suppressed. Priests' conscience-driven shame, Ezra's deep inward shame, Jerusalem's commanded shame before divine grace, shame as the precondition for renewed knowledge of God.

Trust in God as shield against shame: refuge and word. Verses where taking refuge in God, holding to his testimonies, waiting on him, or boldly declaring his word is the present protective faculty against shame. The psalmist's multiple pleas, the linking of blameless heart and God's statutes to shame-avoidance, faith in Christ securing the inner person against disgrace, hope confirmed by the Spirit.

Corrective shame awakening conscience toward God. Verses where shame is deployed as an inward corrective force in the vertical relationship — Paul invoking shame to awaken conscience, distinguishing fatherly correction from shame-induction, shame silencing the self-justifying mouth before atonement, false prophets ashamed of their visions as conscience-corrective, not throwing away confidence before God.

Promise: never put to shame in God's presence. Verses where the covenant or eschatological promise of not being put to shame before God is the primary content — everlasting salvation precluding shame permanently, God's presence guaranteeing freedom from future disgrace, faith in the cornerstone shielding from shame's inner devastation, the exposed inner condition of spiritual poverty covered by Christ's garments.

Social and bodily humiliation: enforced downward movement in human community. Verses where humiliation operates through visible, socially enacted demotion — shaved beards preventing re-entry to public life, expulsion to the lowest place, inward behavioural retreat, the man rendered speechless before an unanswerable challenge, authority figures silenced publicly. The inner shame is inseparable from the outward social sign.

Military defeat and national collapse as humiliation. Verses where humiliation comes through military defeat, the collapse of national power, or the stripping of political identity — cities' defences broken, peoples reduced to withered grass, empires stripped of imperial standing, diviners exposed as incompetent by God's silence. The specific shame of having trusted in strength that was overwhelmed.

Divine abasement of pride: God bringing the lofty low. Verses where humiliation is the direct, sovereign act of God against the inner disposition of pride and self-exaltation — haughty pride laid low so the Lord alone is exalted, the high tree brought down, the turban and crown removed, the moon and sun ashamed before divine glory. Disgrace as the inevitable inner consequence of pride is the wisdom-tradition form of this register.

Shame carried into death and as enduring burden. Verses where humiliation is permanent — warriors descending to the pit bearing their shame, disgrace persisting beyond life, perpetual unforgettable reproach branded on identity, shame restricting priestly access as an ongoing inner-social penalty without end.

Active dishonour: refusing or withdrawing due regard toward persons. Verses where dishonour is actively enacted through willful refusal to acknowledge worth — the judge lacking inner regard for God or persons, accusers willfully dishonoring Jesus, social breakdown producing insolence toward the honourable, hypocrisy concealing contempt for the lawgiver, symbolic acts conveying disrespect toward authority, publicly dishonoring Christ through apostasy, treating the poor as if they had no worth.

Assigned or structural dishonour: standing, nature, and received treatment. Verses where dishonour is assigned, structurally determined, or received as social treatment without alteration of inner integrity — potter's sovereign assignment of vessels, nature teaching social-moral judgment grounded in created order, dishonour as outward treatment received without altering inner integrity, workers protected from the dishonour of unpaid labour.

Shameful conduct and objects: the moral quality of what is shameful. Verses where conscience identifies conduct, speech, actions, or objects as morally shameful — violations of inner propriety, deeds too degraded to name, shameful financial gain, idolatrous objects consuming life, Israel becoming like the shameful object of devotion, renouncing shameful underhanded ways. Mute idols' total absence of will and agency as the paradigm of what is shameful.

Indecent desires and moral disorder: disordered inner appetite. Verses where the shameful characteristic is located in disordered inner desire — dishonorable passions as the inner driving force behind moral disorder, bodily-enacted indecency rooted in corrupted appetite, moral-spiritual unpreparedness made visible as indecency at Christ's coming.

Inverted conscience: glorying in shame and the perverted evaluative faculty. Verses where the moral-evaluation apparatus itself is corrupted — the conscience that treats disgrace as honour, the will that loves what disgraces it, God giving people over to dishonoring their own bodies when conscience is abandoned to sinful desire.

Dismissive contempt as inner attitude: treating persons as of no account. Verses where the contempt-producing-shame mechanism operates through an inner attitude of dismissal — despising fellow believers, devaluing ministry, rejecting Spirit-given speech, contempt enacted through callous disregard of the poor at the Lord's Supper. God choosing the despised overturns human contempt-judgments. Cross-register note (M06): contempt is the source-side; M07 holds the shame produced in the recipient.

Active contempt expressed through violence, mockery, and rejection. Verses where contempt is enacted through physical violence against authority-bearers, public mocking, or willful rejection — servants struck in the vineyard, Herod and soldiers mocking Jesus, the builders' willful dismissal of the cornerstone reversed by God, Bartimaeus abandoning his contempt-formed beggar identity at Jesus's call. Cross-register note (M06): enacted contempt as source-side mechanism.

Verbal contempt and reputation attack as shaming mechanism. Verses where shame is produced through speech — verbal attacks on reputation, hostile reviling, slanderous assault, the scoffer's verbal abuse generating dishonour. Right conduct as the preemptive shield against slander; not returning reviling with reviling; the prophet's irrationality as occasion of vocative reproach. Cross-register note (M06): verbal contempt as source-side.

Innocence as active inner defence: clean conscience before God and persons. Verses where innocence — the integrity of will and conscience, the cleanness of hands reflecting freedom from deliberate wrongdoing — functions as an active inner claim or defence enabling standing before God. Cross-register note (M12): the purity content of these terms (clean hands, washed conscience) is the primary register belonging to M12; M07 holds the innocence-as-structural-counter-to-shame angle.

Innocence questioned and incapacity for innocence. Verses where the innocence-shame polarity is evidenced through the questioning of innocence's protective function or through the incapacity of a people to attain it — maintained inner purity seeming futile when it brings no visible protection, the people incapable of attaining innocence due to persistent idolatry sustaining their shamed condition. Cross-register note (M12): purity-and-consecration content primary in M12.

BOUNDARY terms pending researcher disposition. Aggregates all 27 is_relevant verses of M07's five BOUNDARY-verdict terms. Primary analytical question: sha.phel (24v) — M07 enforced-humiliation-of-pride register vs M09 voluntary-lowliness-of-spirit register. Additional items: mish.chat (1v, outward physical mutilation only), ni.dah (1v, M07/M12 filth-shame boundary), katatomē (1v, minimal inner-being content), te.val.lul (0v, data gap). Phase 8.5 disposition will determine final routing of each term.
